bytes.topic: Testing Strategy — unit vs integration vs E2E pyramid, what to test at each level
bytes.title: Playwright Testing Pyramid — What to Test at Each Layer
bytes.body:
Playwright operates at the E2E layer, but that doesn't mean every test should be E2E. Structure tests in three tiers.

**Unit (skip Playwright):** Pure logic — date formatters, validators, state reducers. Use Vitest or Jest. Playwright adds zero value here and only slows CI.

**Component/Integration (use Playwright CT):** `@playwright/experimental-ct-react` lets you mount isolated components inside a real browser. Test a `<DatePicker />` keyboard navigation, ARIA roles, and focus trapping without a full app. Faster than full E2E, catches browser-specific DOM quirks that jsdom misses.

**E2E (full Playwright):** Reserve for critical user journeys — auth flows, checkout, form submissions with network calls. Use `page.route()` to stub flaky third-party APIs, keeping tests deterministic. Rely on `locator()` with ARIA roles (`getByRole('button', { name: 'Submit' })`) over CSS selectors to mirror how users and screen readers perceive the UI.

A healthy ratio: ~70% unit, ~20% component, ~10% E2E. Playwright CT is the underused middle layer most teams skip entirely.

bytes.topic: Test Behaviour Not Implementation — Testing Library philosophy, query by role not by class
bytes.title: Playwright — Test Behaviour Not Implementation: Query by Role, Not by Class
bytes.body:
Playwright's Locator API encourages behaviour-driven selectors that survive refactors. When you query by CSS class or test ID, you're coupling tests to implementation details — a designer renaming `.btn-primary` breaks your suite without breaking the app.

Instead, reach for `getByRole()`, `getByLabel()`, `getByText()`, and `getByPlaceholder()`. These mirror how real users perceive the UI, and how assistive technologies traverse it. A button is a button because of its ARIA role, not its class name.

`getByRole('button', { name: 'Submit' })` finds exactly the element a screen reader would announce as "Submit button" — regardless of whether the underlying element is a `<button>`, `<input type="submit">`, or a custom component with `role="button"`.

This philosophy also improves accessibility coverage for free: if `getByRole` can't find it, neither can a screen reader. Pair it with `toBeVisible()` and `toBeEnabled()` assertions to verify observable behaviour, not DOM structure. Reserve `data-testid` only when no semantic query is possible.

bytes.topic: Component Testing — rendering, user interaction, asserting on visible output
bytes.title: Playwright Component Testing — Render, Interact, Assert Without a Full Browser App
bytes.body:
Playwright's experimental `@playwright/experimental-ct-react` (or Vue/Svelte variants) lets you mount individual components in isolation — no dev server, no routing, no full app shell. You call `mount()` from `@playwright/experimental-ct-react`, pass props directly, and get back a `Locator`-compatible handle. From there, every standard Playwright API works exactly as in E2E tests: `click()`, `fill()`, `expect(component).toHaveText()`, `toBeVisible()`, `toHaveAttribute()`.

The key difference from E2E: you control props and slots at the test boundary, so you never need to navigate or seed a database to exercise a component's visual states. Use `component.update({ props })` to re-render with new props mid-test and assert on the resulting DOM without a page reload. For interaction testing, Playwright's actionability checks (auto-wait for visible + enabled) still apply — `click()` won't fire on a disabled button, keeping your assertions honest. Slot/children injection works too, letting you test compound components like `<Dialog>` without mocking a trigger.

bytes.topic: Unit Testing — pure functions, custom hooks with renderHook
bytes.title: Playwright Component Testing — Testing Custom Hooks Without renderHook
bytes.body:
Playwright's `@playwright/experimental-ct-react` ships no `renderHook` utility. Instead, build a minimal HookHarness component that exposes your hook's return values through testable DOM nodes, then mount it with Playwright's `mount()`. This runs inside a real Chromium, Firefox, or WebKit process — not jsdom — which catches browser-specific edge cases that RTL misses entirely.

For pure functions with no browser dependencies, call them directly in the test body; no mounting overhead required. For functions that touch browser APIs — `localStorage`, `matchMedia`, `ResizeObserver`, `requestAnimationFrame` — use `page.evaluate()` to execute them in browser context with full native API access.

The critical difference from RTL's `renderHook` is environment fidelity. A hook wrapping `IntersectionObserver` or `Web Workers` fires against real browser internals, not stubs. jsdom's `IntersectionObserver` mock diverges from Chrome's threshold logic; Playwright doesn't lie. The tradeoff is speed — Playwright component tests run 5–10× slower than Jest. Reserve them for hooks with genuine browser dependencies; use Vitest for the rest.

bytes.topic: Integration Testing — testing multiple components together, mocking at the right boundary
bytes.title: Playwright Integration Testing — Mocking at the Right Boundary
bytes.body:
Integration tests in Playwright verify that multiple components work together — a form submission that triggers an API call and updates the UI. The key decision is where to mock. Mock too low (individual functions) and you're unit testing. Mock too high (the entire backend) and you miss real integration failures.

The right boundary is the network layer. Use `page.route()` to intercept HTTP requests at the fetch/XHR level, letting your actual React components, state management, and DOM interactions run unmodified. This validates the full frontend slice — component rendering, event handlers, state updates — while keeping tests deterministic.

For component composition tests, avoid `page.evaluate()` stubs. Instead, seed the network mock with realistic response shapes matching your actual API contract. If you use MSW in development, reuse those same handlers via `playwright-msw` to keep mocks DRY across test environments.

Use `page.waitForResponse()` to assert the correct request was made before checking UI state — this avoids brittle `waitForTimeout()` calls and explicitly documents the async dependency.

bytes.topic: E2E Testing — full user flows, critical paths only (auth, onboarding, core feature)
bytes.title: Playwright E2E — Testing Critical Paths Without Drowning in Flake
bytes.body:
E2E tests are expensive — run them selectively on paths that break users, not every UI interaction. In Playwright, structure critical flows as isolated, stateless tests using `storageState` to bypass auth on every run. Authenticate once in a `globalSetup`, serialize the session to a JSON file, and load it per-test via `use.storageState` in `playwright.config.ts`. This eliminates the login flow from every test while still covering it in a dedicated auth spec.

For onboarding and core feature flows, use `page.getByRole()` and `page.getByLabel()` over CSS selectors — they survive UI refactors and mirror how users interact with the page. Chain `expect(page).toHaveURL()` at route transitions to assert navigation without arbitrary waits.

Scope each test to one critical path. Auth spec owns login/logout/session-expiry. Onboarding spec owns the setup wizard end-to-end. Core feature spec owns the primary happy path. Tag them with `@critical` and run only these in CI pre-merge; full suite runs nightly. This keeps your pipeline fast and your signal high.

bytes.topic: Mocking — MSW for API mocking, when to mock vs use real dependencies
bytes.title: Playwright API Mocking — MSW Handlers vs page.route() and When Real Deps Win
bytes.body:
Playwright ships two mocking primitives: `page.route()` for test-local interception and `route.fulfill()` to return fixtures inline. These are fine for one-offs, but they don't share handlers with your unit tests. That's where `playwright-msw` earns its place — it mounts real MSW service workers inside the Playwright browser context, so the same handler definitions used in Vitest or Jest run verbatim in E2E. This eliminates drift between mock layers.

Use mocks when: third-party APIs have rate limits, you're testing loading/error UI states that are hard to reproduce reliably, or you need deterministic latency with `worker.use(http.get(..., { delay: 200 }))`. Use real dependencies when: you're verifying a write path end-to-end (auth token exchange, database mutation with RLS, payment webhook), or when the mock would underspecify the integration contract. Mock the edges you don't own; test the system you do. In Playwright specifically, `page.route()` can intercept even service-worker-forwarded requests, so it remains your escape hatch when MSW handler order matters.

bytes.topic: Async Testing — waitFor, findBy queries, handling promises and fake timers
bytes.title: Playwright Async Testing — waitFor, Auto-Waiting, and Taming Fake Timers
bytes.body:
Playwright's auto-waiting bakes assertions directly into locator actions — `locator.click()` waits for the element to be visible, stable, and enabled before acting. But for custom conditions, `page.waitForFunction()` lets you poll arbitrary JS: `await page.waitForFunction(() => window.__dataLoaded === true)`. For network-driven UI updates, `page.waitForResponse()` or `page.waitForRequest()` give you precise control without arbitrary timeouts.

Locator assertions via `expect(locator).toBeVisible()` retry internally up to the configured `timeout` — no manual polling needed. Avoid `page.waitForTimeout()` entirely; it's a code smell that masks real flakiness.

Fake timers are where Playwright diverges sharply from Jest. You install them via `page.clock.install()` (Playwright 1.45+), then advance with `page.clock.fastForward(5000)` or `page.clock.runFor(3000)`. This controls `setTimeout`, `setInterval`, and `Date` inside the browser context — not the Node test runner. Combine with `expect(locator).toHaveText()` after advancing the clock to assert debounced UI updates deterministically.

bytes.topic: Snapshot Testing — when it helps vs when it becomes noise
bytes.title: Playwright Snapshot Testing — When It Helps vs When It Becomes Noise
bytes.body:
Playwright offers two snapshot flavors: `toMatchSnapshot()` for pixel-level screenshots and `toHaveScreenshot()` (the modern, preferred API) for visual regression with built-in retry and threshold options. They shine for catching unintended UI regressions in stable, deterministic components — charts, design system primitives, marketing pages. The failure signal is high when the component has no dynamic content.

The noise starts when you snapshot components with timestamps, user avatars, animated elements, or data from live APIs. Every CI run produces a diff, but none of it is a real regression. The fix isn't to widen `maxDiffPixels` blindly — it's to mask dynamic regions with `page.locator('.timestamp').evaluate(el => el.textContent = '...')` before snapshotting, or to use `clip` to isolate only the stable portion of the viewport.

Update snapshots intentionally with `--update-snapshots`, never as a reflex. Treat a snapshot update the same as a schema migration: review it in the PR diff. Store `.png` baselines in version control but keep them co-located with the test file via the `snapshotDir` config to avoid orphaned files accumulating silently.

bytes.topic: Visual Regression — Chromatic with Storybook, screenshot diffing in CI
bytes.title: Playwright Visual Regression — toHaveScreenshot, Chromatic, and CI Diffing
bytes.body:
Playwright's built-in visual regression uses `expect(page).toHaveScreenshot()`, which stores `.png` baselines under `__snapshots__` and pixel-diffs on every subsequent run. Tune sensitivity with `{ maxDiffPixels: 50 }` or `{ threshold: 0.2 }` (ratio of differing pixels) — tighter thresholds catch sub-pixel regressions, looser ones survive anti-aliasing noise across OS renderers.

For component-level coverage, `@chromatic-com/playwright` ships a `takeSnapshot` helper that pipes Playwright screenshots into Chromatic's cloud diffing engine, giving you a PR-gated UI review workflow without managing baseline files in git.

In CI, never commit stale baselines. Instead, gate on `--update-snapshots` only on a dedicated baseline branch, then treat snapshot mismatches as hard failures on feature branches. Use `snapshotPathTemplate` in `playwright.config.ts` to namespace baselines by OS and browser (`{testFileDir}/__snapshots__/{platform}/{browserName}/{arg}{ext}`), preventing Linux CI from invalidating macOS baselines and vice versa. Combine Playwright's native diffing for full-page flows with Chromatic for isolated Storybook components — they solve different granularities of the same problem.

bytes.topic: Accessibility Testing — axe-core integration, automated a11y checks in tests
bytes.title: Playwright + axe-core — Automated Accessibility Audits in Every Test
bytes.body:
Playwright has no built-in a11y engine, but `@axe-core/playwright` plugs directly into any test via the `AxeBuilder` class. After navigating to a page, construct an `AxeBuilder` with the current `page`, call `.analyze()`, and assert against `results.violations`. Each violation carries `id`, `impact`, `nodes`, and `helpUrl` — enough context to fix the issue without leaving the terminal.

Scope audits with `.include('#main-content')` to skip third-party widgets you can't control, or `.exclude('.legacy-widget')` to suppress known noise. Use `.withTags(['wcag2a', 'wcag2aa'])` to target a specific conformance level rather than running every axe rule on every page load — this cuts runtime and keeps CI failures actionable.

Pair this with Playwright's `--reporter=html` so violations are surfaced inline alongside screenshots and traces. For component-level coverage, run the same `AxeBuilder` pattern inside `@playwright/experimental-ct-react` tests to catch a11y regressions before they reach a full page.

bytes.topic: Test Data Management — factories, fixtures, seeding test state
bytes.title: Playwright Test Data Management — Factories, Fixtures, and Seeding State
bytes.body:
Hardcoding test data inline breeds brittle tests. Playwright's fixture system is the right hook for centralized test data management. Define a `userFactory` that generates deterministic or randomized user objects, then wire it into a custom `test` fixture that seeds your database via API calls in `beforeEach` and tears down in `afterEach` — all scoped per-test or per-worker.

Use `test.extend()` to compose fixtures: a `seededUser` fixture can depend on a `request` fixture to hit your `/api/test/seed` endpoint, returning a typed object your test consumes directly. Worker-scoped fixtures (`scope: 'worker'`) amortize expensive seeds — like a pre-authenticated session — across the full worker, while test-scoped fixtures isolate mutable state.

For UI-heavy flows, combine `page.goto()` with `storageState` snapshots: seed auth state once, serialize it with `context.storageState()`, then reuse it across tests via `use: { storageState: 'state.json' }` in `playwright.config.ts`. This eliminates redundant login flows while keeping state deterministic and isolated per test run.

bytes.topic: CI Integration — parallelizing tests, caching, fail-fast strategies
bytes.title: Playwright CI Integration — Parallelizing Tests, Caching, and Fail-Fast Strategies
bytes.body:
Playwright's built-in sharding splits your test suite across CI workers without any external orchestration. Set `--shard=1/4` through `--shard=4/4` across four parallel jobs, then merge results with `npx playwright merge-reports`. Each shard runs a distinct subset of spec files, cutting wall-clock time linearly.

Cache the `.playwright-browsers` directory (resolved via `npx playwright install --dry-run`) keyed on the Playwright version in `package-lock.json`. Browser binaries rarely change — skipping re-downloads saves 30–90 seconds per run.

For fail-fast behavior, Playwright doesn't expose a native `--bail` flag, but `maxFailures` in `playwright.config.ts` stops the run after N failures across all workers. Set `maxFailures: 1` in CI to abort immediately on the first flake, preventing wasted worker minutes. Combine with `retries: 0` in CI (non-zero retries mask flakiness) and `reporter: [['github'], ['blob']]` so GitHub Actions annotates failing lines directly. Use the `blob` reporter per shard, then `merge-reports` to produce a unified HTML report as a CI artifact.

bytes.topic: Vitest-specific — vite-native speed, compatible Jest API, in-source testing
bytes.title: Playwright Component Testing — Vite-Native Speed, Jest-Compatible Assertions, In-Source Test Files
bytes.body: Playwright's component testing (`@playwright/experimental-ct-react`) runs real browser interactions but is Vite-powered under the hood. Edit a component and the test re-runs via HMR without a full browser restart — same sub-second feedback loop you get with Vitest for unit tests. The `expect` API is intentionally Jest/Vitest-compatible: `await expect(component).toBeVisible()` and `await expect(component).toContainText('Submit')` mirror Vitest's chainable interface, so assertion muscle memory transfers directly. The key asymmetry: Playwright CT assertions are always async and locator-based, while Vitest assertions on DOM nodes are synchronous — mixing both in one repo means `await` discipline matters. Configure `testMatch` in `playwright-ct.config.ts` to `**/*.ct.spec.tsx` inside `src/`, placing component tests next to the source files they cover. This in-source layout is the same pattern Vitest encourages for unit tests. The result: Vitest handles pure logic co-located with source, Playwright CT handles interaction fidelity co-located with components — both sharing Vite's module graph and the same assertion vocabulary.

bytes.topic: Playwright-specific — multi-browser, trace viewer, network interception
bytes.title: Playwright's Trace Viewer + Network Interception: Debug Failures Like a Pro
bytes.body:
When a Playwright test fails in CI, the trace viewer is your first stop — not console logs. Enable it via `use: { trace: 'on-first-retry' }` in `playwright.config.ts`, then open `npx playwright show-trace trace.zip` to replay every action, snapshot, and network request with a timeline scrubber.

Pair this with `page.route()` to intercept and mock network calls before they hit the wire. You can return fixture JSON for slow or flaky third-party APIs, simulate 500 errors to test error boundaries, or abort specific asset requests to speed up tests. Routes are matched by glob or regex against the full URL.

For multi-browser coverage, use the `projects` array in config to run the same spec against `chromium`, `firefox`, and `webkit` in parallel — each project can override `use` settings, so you can enable video recording only for WebKit. Run a single project with `--project=webkit` during local debugging to avoid full suite overhead. The trace viewer is browser-agnostic and works identically across all three engines.

bytes.title: Playwright page.route() — Intercepting, Mocking, and Overriding Network Requests
bytes.body:
`page.route()` is Playwright's network interception primitive — register a glob or regex pattern and intercept every matching request before it hits the wire. Unlike Cypress's `cy.intercept`, `page.route()` is async by design: your handler receives a `Route` object and must settle it with exactly one of `route.fulfill()`, `route.abort()`, or `route.continue()`.

`route.fulfill()` returns arbitrary status codes, headers, and bodies — ideal for mocking REST or GraphQL endpoints without a running server. `route.abort()` simulates failure conditions (`failed`, `timedout`, `connectionreset`). `route.continue()` passes the request through, letting you selectively rewrite headers or the post body mid-flight.

When you need per-test overrides, register a second `page.route()` for the same pattern — Playwright resolves the most recently registered handler first, so you get clean, teardown-free overrides on top of a `beforeEach` baseline.

To assert on outgoing requests, capture them inside the handler via `route.request()` or pair with `page.waitForRequest()`. For deterministic fixture-driven tests, `page.routeFromHAR()` replays recorded sessions verbatim — no manual stub maintenance required.

bytes.topic: Storybook — component documentation, interaction testing, story-driven development
bytes.title: Playwright + Storybook — Your Stories Are Already Integration Tests
bytes.body:
Storybook's test runner is powered by Playwright under the hood. When you define a `play` function in a story, Playwright navigates to that story's isolated iframe, executes the interaction sequence, and surfaces failures as proper test cases. Your documentation and your test suite are the same artifact.

The `.storybook/test-runner.ts` config exposes Playwright's `page` object directly through `preVisit` and `postVisit` hooks. This gives you the full Playwright API per story — `page.screenshot()` for visual regression baselines, `page.evaluate()` for DOM state inspection, or `axe-playwright` for a11y auditing across every component variant.

For CI: `storybook build && npx test-storybook --url http://localhost:6006`. Add `--shard 1/4` to parallelize since each story maps to an independent Playwright page visit.

One sharp edge: `userEvent` inside `play` functions uses `@testing-library/user-event` — not Playwright's native input engine. For precise pointer coordinates or drag sequences, reach for `page.mouse.move()` and `page.mouse.down()` inside `postVisit` instead.

bytes.topic: Code Coverage — what it tells you and what it doesn't, avoiding coverage theater
bytes.title: Playwright Code Coverage — What It Tells You and What It Doesn't
bytes.body:
Playwright's `page.coverage` API (Chromium-only) tracks which JavaScript byte ranges executed during a test. It's useful for finding dead code. What it cannot do is tell you whether your assertions are meaningful.

A test that calls `page.goto('/checkout')` and clicks through a happy path can report 85% JS coverage while your error boundary, retry logic, and empty-state branches remain completely untested. Coverage measures execution, not assertion quality.

Three traps to avoid:

**Coverage ≠ confidence.** A test with zero `expect()` calls can hit 100% coverage. Playwright executes your app's JS; it doesn't evaluate whether the output was correct.

**Branch blindness.** V8 coverage reports byte ranges, not branch outcomes. An `if/else` where only the `if` arm ran looks identical to one where both ran — unless you convert via `v8-to-istanbul` and inspect Istanbul's branch map explicitly.

**Navigation resets.** `startJSCoverage()` resets counters on each navigation by default. Pass `{ resetOnNavigation: false }` to accumulate coverage across a multi-page flow, or you'll silently undercount.

Use coverage as a deletion metric: it reliably identifies untouched modules. It says nothing about whether the touched code was tested well.
